summary: DeVault catalogs his long history of botching software releases and the incremental automation he built to prevent each class of mistake. Starting from fully manual releases, he describes a parade of failures — wrong version numbers, bad changelogs, forgotten version bumps, missing scripts, incompatible toolchains, releasing from feature branches, and pushing stale tags. Each mistake led to a new safeguard in his semver script, but each fix only revealed the next creative way to screw up. The post is a honest, self-deprecating chronicle of how release engineering is an endless arms race against human error.
key_points: Manual release processes inevitably produce mistakes, and each automation fix only reveals the next failure mode
Version bumps, changelogs, signing, branch checks, and pull synchronization all need scripting because humans will forget each one in turn
Environment differences (Busybox vs GNU gzip) can silently produce mismatched artifacts, breaking reproducibility
Scripts stored outside version control are a ticking time bomb — they work until you change machines
Release engineering is an ongoing arms race where you can never fully outsmart your own capacity for mistakes
money_quotes: Just about every time I ship a release, I find a novel way to fuck it up.
No matter how much I scripted away my mistakes I would always find a new and novel way of screwing up.
I am doomed to creatively outsmart my tools in releases.
I would frequently fuck this up in many creative ways: forgetting it entirely; updating it but not committing it; updating it and committing it, but tagging the wrong commit.
On the very next release I shipped, I mistakenly did everything from a feature branch, and shipped experimental, incomplete code in a stable release.
I tagged a release without pulling first, and when I pushed, git happily rejected my branch and accepted the tag - shipping an outdated commit as the release.
key_insight: Release engineering is an endless arms race against human error, where every automated safeguard you add only reveals the next creative way you'll manage to screw things up.
